interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Secretary and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

main_summary.summary:
Dorothy 'Dottie' Zellner recounts her life from her birth in New York City on January 14, 1938, to her decades of civil rights activism. Born to Jewish immigrant parents (her father from Lithuania via England and Toronto, her mother from Kiev via Toronto), she grew up in a left-wing household preoccupied by World War II, the Holocaust, and McCarthyism. She describes formative experiences including reading 'The Black Book' on the concentration camps as a child, the execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg (which she protested in front of the White House at age 15), the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising as a moral touchstone, and the arrest of a Communist Party neighbor under the Smith Act in 1951.

Electrified by news of the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Zellner attended a CORE nonviolence workshop in Miami in June 1960, where she was arrested at a lunch counter sit-in with a group including Patricia and Priscilla Stevens, Angeline Butler, Hugh Murray, Rudy Lombard, and Bernard Lafayette. Bernard Lafayette directed her toward SNCC. She traveled with the CORE contingent to New Orleans for the city's first sit-ins, where she did outreach to Father Joseph Fichter at Loyola University (who agreed to help) and to Rabbi Julian Feibelman (who threw her out of his office). After working briefly for the Southern Regional Council in Atlanta beginning June 1961 under James A. Moss, she met James Forman at SNCC in October 1961 and began volunteering. Forman put her to work typing affidavits from field staff that were sent to Robert F. Kennedy, then partnered her with Julian Bond in SNCC communications.

Zellner describes James Forman as SNCC's organizational architect — the inventor of the 'friends of SNCC' support-group model, the hub-and-wheel structure, and the discipline of sending staff for rest and recuperation. She recounts how Forman saved her life twice: once during a police stop near Greenwood, Mississippi after the 1963 folk festival featuring Bob Dylan and Pete Seeger, and once in Danville, Virginia during a 1963 demonstration at City Hall where she had been struck on the head by a police officer's stick during an earlier action by the jail. She married Bob Zellner in August 1963 in Atlanta. She established a SNCC Northeast Regional office in Boston / Cambridge while Bob attended graduate school, vetted Mississippi Summer Project volunteers (including, inadvertently, Abbie Hoffman), and worked the WATS line in Greenwood during Freedom Summer 1964. She gave birth to her daughter Margaret in July 1965, took a leave during 1966 when Bob managed Robert Cook's anti–Vietnam War congressional campaign in New Haven, and returned to SNCC.

In May 1967 at the Peg Leg Bates staff meeting, Zellner and Bob submitted a written proposal — which she drafted — to remain on SNCC staff and organize in the white community while retaining a vote in the organization; the proposal was rejected. They moved to New Orleans to work for Anne Braden at the Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) for five years. She remembers Ruby Doris Smith Robinson (whom she visited at Beth Israel Hospital during Robinson's fatal illness), Julian Bond (whose death she mourns deeply), and the 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop with Anne Braden and Ella Baker that decided SNCC would not require loyalty oaths and would take the First Amendment rather than the Fifth if subpoenaed. She closes by reflecting that SNCC achieved a 'non-racial community' in the South African sense and that its dissolution was a tragedy for the country; she now works as a Jewish activist on Israel/Palestine issues, drawing on the lessons SNCC taught her.
key_themes: Jewish radical heritage and the Holocaust as motivation for civil rights work
McCarthyism, the Smith Act, and Cold War repression
Women's roles and constraints in SNCC organizing
Interracial organizing and the danger faced by white women working with Black men in the South
James Forman's organizational genius and the hub-and-spoke 'friends of SNCC' model
The 1967 Peg Leg Bates decision and white staff departure from SNCC
Continuity from civil rights organizing to later activism (Israel/Palestine)
main_summary.historical_significance: This interview is a rare extended firsthand account from a white Jewish woman who served on SNCC's central staff from 1961 through 1967, spanning the period from the loft office on Auburn Avenue through the post–Peg Leg Bates reorganization. Zellner offers granular insider perspective on James Forman's administrative inventions (the friends-of-SNCC support model, the field-affidavit system feeding Robert F. Kennedy's Justice Department, the practice of sending organizers for R&R), the 1963 civil-liberties workshop that protected SNCC from McCarthyite investigation, the volunteer-vetting process for Mississippi Summer, and the internal debate over white staff that culminated at Peg Leg Bates in May 1967. Her recollections of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's illness, Julian Bond as her writing partner, and her own near-deaths in Greenwood and Danville add texture rarely available in the published historiography, and her dual identity as Jewish activist provides a comparative lens on movement organizing that bridges 1960s civil rights and contemporary Israel/Palestine activism.

errors: The transcript renders Joseph Fichter's institution as 'Loyola University New Orleans'; this is correct (Loyola University New Orleans existed), but Zellner's own initial phrasing in the transcript was 'Loyola University New Orleans, Loyola University New Orleans' — care taken to use canonical name without paraphrasing into 'Loyola of the South' or similar. Summary text is faithful.

The summary states Zellner's daughter Margaret was born in July 1965 — the transcript supports 'July' as birth month and 1965 as year by inference from Zellner's statement that she got pregnant in 1965 and her daughter 'was born in July', combined with the framing of 'fall of 65' return to Atlanta. The exact year requires reconstructing from context; flagged as a chronological inference rather than a direct citation.

The summary attributes the police stop to occurring 'near Greenwood, Mississippi after the 1963 folk festival featuring Bob Dylan and Pete Seeger.' The transcript says Greenwood and mentions Bob Dylan + Pete Seeger at the festival but Zellner herself expresses uncertainty about whether it was 1963 — flagged as 'I'm trying to think... It was a tree, but I'm not sure'.
improvements: Tighten the chronological scaffolding where Zellner's own narration is uncertain (e.g., the Greenwood folk festival year, the exact date of the Peg Leg Bates meeting).
Clarify that the 1957 Civil Rights Act she references for federal voter-registration enforcement is correct (1957 Civil Rights Act did establish federal voting-rights enforcement mechanisms).
Note that Constance 'Dinky' Romilly is consistently referred to as 'Dinky Forman' by Zellner — preserve both names per Zellner's usage rather than imposing 'Romilly'.

chapters.title: New York Childhood, Jewish Immigrant Family, and World War II
chapters.summary: Zellner describes being born in New York City on January 14, 1938, the oldest of three children of immigrant Jewish parents — her father from Lithuania via England and Toronto, her mother from Kiev via Toronto. Her father became a dentist (barred from teaching in anti-Semitic Canada) and emigrated to the US in 1929. The family lived in a six-floor apartment building with a fire escape, with her father's dental office in the front rooms and the family in three rooms in the back. Both parents were left-wing and secular Jews. Zellner describes World War II as 'another member of the family,' her father obsessively listening to radio war news between patients, her own terror upon seeing a newsreel of a war-wounded child, and her discovery as an eight-year-old of 'The Black Book' depicting the concentration camps in her parents' library. She recalls early racial consciousness — at age five, loudly asking her mother on a New York bus why Black passengers weren't speaking to each other ('I thought they were all in the same family'). Her father treated Spanish Civil War veterans for free for the rest of his life and later did the same for SNCC field workers, including taking the wires out of Bill Hansen's jaw after he was beaten in jail (likely in Arkansas).

improvements: Could expand on the Bill Hansen jaw-wiring vignette to clarify it occurred during a SNCC-era civil rights jailing, not childhood.

chapters.title: McCarthyism, the Smith Act, and the Rosenberg Execution
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the McCarthy era through her teenage life. A leading member of the Communist Party lived in her apartment building and was arrested at seven AM in 1951 under the Smith Act, which she explains made it a crime to 'conspire and advocate the overthrow of the U.S. government by force and violence' — 'two steps removed, at least, from actual violence,' and none of those convicted ever possessed 'a pea shooter, much less a gun.' She describes the climate as one in which her high-school class's proposal to invite J. Robert Oppenheimer as 1955 graduation speaker was rejected as 'outlandish.' The signal event of her teenage life was the June 1953 execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg; she traveled to Washington with her friend Freddie (son of the Communist Party family in her building), Freddie's mother, and her own mother. When the Supreme Court issued a stay, she stayed in Washington and picketed all night in front of the White House at age 15. She remembers picketing alongside Mary Travers, later of Peter, Paul and Mary, who was then 'a couple of years older.' She describes herself as living on 'books and stories about the war,' particularly the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising — six weeks of armed resistance by Jews who knew they would die — which became a lifelong moral touchstone for her: 'if you are up against the fascists... you must bring as many of them as you can.'
related_events: Execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg (1953)

improvements: Add brief contextual note on what the Smith Act was for non-specialist readers (already partially done).

chapters.title: Greensboro Sit-Ins and the Miami CORE Workshop
chapters.summary: On February 2, 1960, the day after the Greensboro Four sat in at Woolworth's, Zellner read a small story in the New York Times while sitting at a coffee-shop counter on 42nd Street. She describes herself as 'electrified': 'it wasn't so much that they couldn't be served. It was that they didn't get up.' Through a friend named Jocelyn (girlfriend of Freddie's friend) who worked at the CORE office, she enrolled in a CORE nonviolence workshop in Miami, Florida in June 1960. The workshop included Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, the Stevens twins (Patricia 'Pat' and Priscilla Stevens), Angeline Butler from Nashville, Jim Lowy, Bernard Lafayette, and national CORE figures including Marvin Rich. Zellner describes herself at the workshop as the only white woman in a group arrested at a lunch counter — singing on the way out as she 'dawned on me that I was the only white woman in the group.' In jail, the matron handed her a 30-pound mop and other inmates (white women on minor charges) mocked her housework skills; she remembers being terrified but not menaced. She bonded out within a day rather than staying. She describes nonviolence training role-plays where participants alternated as victims and 'perps' — and her own discomfort at how easy it was to scream slurs once cast as the perpetrator: 'you can turn into a fascist, you know, unless you're careful.'

improvements: Note that the transcript renders 'Pat' as 'Patricia' in confirming dialogue — preserved both forms.

chapters.title: First Sit-In in New Orleans and Outreach to White Religious Communities
chapters.summary: After the Miami workshop, Zellner traveled with the CORE contingent to New Orleans for one to two weeks, staying with a Black woman whose name she does not recall on or near Claiborne Avenue. The New Orleans contingent was preparing the city's first sit-ins; Zellner notes the unusual fact that despite the presence of Xavier (a historically Black Catholic college) and other institutions, sit-ins had not yet occurred there, and the impetus appeared to come from CORE rather than from local students. Her assignments included making signs (she had attended Music and Art High School), casing the Woolworth's on Canal Street, and conducting white-community outreach. She visited Joseph Fichter, a well-known Loyola University New Orleans priest noted for his racial-justice work, who agreed his parishes would help. She then visited Rabbi Julian Feibelman (F-E-I-B-E-L-M-A-N), who threw her out of his office — a story she has told in talks 'for 55 years.' During the sit-in itself she was assigned to drive a getaway car despite being a terrible new driver, and nearly got the occupants killed at a traffic circle. The sit-in itself was dramatic: Hugh Murray's father heard on the radio that a sit-in was underway with a white participant, rushed in, broke through police lines, grabbed his son, and dragged him from the Woolworth's counter.
related_events: Catholic Church integration in New Orleans

errors: The exact name 'Xavier' is given by Zellner without 'University of Louisiana' — preserved as transcribed.
improvements: Clarify Loyola of the South vs. Loyola Marymount — Loyola University New Orleans is the correct institution per transcript.

chapters.title: From Southern Regional Council to SNCC: Meeting James Forman
chapters.summary: Returning to New York, Zellner held a brief proofreading job for a scientific magazine and then a position as a welfare investigator. By a coincidence — James A. Moss, who had been the only Black professor at Queens College when she was student-newspaper editor and whose contract had not been renewed, was now research director at the Southern Regional Council — her job application reached him, and within three weeks she was in Atlanta. Beginning June 1961 she worked as a research associate at SRC, doing the research and clipping work that produced the SRC's first major report on the eighteen-month sit-in movement (heavily edited by Leslie Dunbar, with Zellner's name unattributed per the conventions of the time). She lived on Peachtree Street on Atlanta's white side and later moved to 2222 Telfair Street on the Black side with Jane Bond as her housemate. In October 1961 — five months after starting at SRC — she walked the twenty-minute distance to the SNCC office for the first time with Swedish journalist Pär Lorsen. James Forman had arrived a month earlier. He shook her hand and asked 'Can you type?' She said yes, was 'beyond thrilled,' and was put to work typing field affidavits that Forman then mailed to Robert F. Kennedy. A few weeks later she told Forman she could also write; he assigned her to work with Julian Bond on the Student Voice newsletter and SNCC communications.

improvements: Confirm spelling 'Pär Lorsen' — the transcript renders it phonetically 'Pair Lorsen.'

chapters.title: Working with James Forman: Two Near-Death Encounters
chapters.summary: Zellner offers an extended portrait of James Forman as the architect of SNCC's organizational infrastructure. She describes his 'switchboard' memory of the organization, his invention of the 'friends of' support-group model (which she notes has since been replicated worldwide), his hub-and-wheel structure with Atlanta as center, his protection of SNCC's autonomy continuing Ella Baker's work, and his insistence on documentation and publicity for movement work rather than himself. She describes two occasions when Forman saved her life. First, after the 1963 Greenwood folk festival featuring Bob Dylan (whom she remembers performing 'With God on Our Side' to a deeply religious audience for whom the title may have struck wrongly) and Pete Seeger, an interracial group of cars was stopped at night by police near a railroad track; Forman handled the stop calmly and without provocation. Second, in Danville, Virginia (Dan River Mills town) during a 1963 demonstration: Zellner had previously been struck on the head by a police officer's club at the jail (the moment captured in Danny Lyon's photograph of Forman taking her affidavit afterward — she insists she was holding her head, not 'posing as a model'), and SNCC returned for a second action at City Hall. When fire hoses and 'deputized drunks' were arrayed against forty people on the steps, Forman distracted the police chief while gesturing behind his back for SNCC to retreat, saving the group. She describes Forman's later mental-health crisis (his first breakdown coming in 1968, with later collapses leading to a chronic-state stabilization after he refused treatment, and his late shift toward supporting figures like Ed Koch that distressed Zellner), his rescue by Constance 'Dinky' Romilly (mother of his sons James Forman Jr., now a Yale law professor, and Shaka, an actor/teacher in California), and Elizabeth 'Betita' Sutherland Martínez's work with him in Puerto Rico on his book. She decries portrayals of Forman in books and in the film 'Selma' as 'petulant' or 'jealous,' insisting his judgment was sound and his contribution profound.

errors: Zellner herself expresses uncertainty about whether the folk festival was 1963 ('It was a tree, but I'm not sure why I'm in 60'). The summary frames it as 1963 per scholarly consensus on the Greenwood festival but flagged as inferred.
improvements: Verify the chronology of Forman's first breakdown ('68' per Zellner) against published Forman biographies.

chapters.title: Working with Julian Bond on SNCC Communications
chapters.summary: Zellner began working with Julian Bond in winter 1961–62 and describes their partnership as effective despite opposite temperaments — she 'hard-driven' and a clock-watcher who arrived at nine when she said nine, he relaxed and conflict-averse. She speaks at length about Bond's recent death and her grief. They produced the Student Voice newsletter (he brought it to a Black newspaper, possibly the Atlanta Inquirer, for typesetting and photo work since they could not do those themselves) and handled SNCC press contacts. She describes their press strategy: always tell the truth even to reporters they disliked; never inflate crowd numbers ('don't say there were 500 people when there were 42 people because the guy who covers it the next time he's not going to come'); deliberately identify Black SNCC leadership in press releases by race ('we did it deliberately because we wanted people to know that they were Black people in the front of this') while leaving white as the implicit default — a practice she notes will look strange to future historians without explanation. She emphasizes Bond's exceptional wit, his early gift for the 'live phone' interview (radio quotes), and her deep gratitude that he defended her in a SNCC staff meeting when she was criticized for her clock-driven punctuality, despite his own conflict-aversion.

chapters.title: Marriage to Bob Zellner, Boston SNCC, and Freedom Summer 1964
chapters.summary: Zellner married Bob Zellner in August 1963 in Atlanta. After the wedding, they moved to Cambridge, Massachusetts, where Bob attended graduate school. Zellner established the SNCC Northeast Regional office — she emphasizes it was a regional office of staff members taking direction from Atlanta, not a Friends-of-SNCC volunteer group like Chicago's. From Boston she interviewed prospective Mississippi Summer Project volunteers using a questionnaire prepared by COFO, working alongside a Black colleague to vet each applicant for adventure-seekers and divas, asking concrete questions like 'Are you going to wear shorts to church?' She recalls a recent reunion where a woman approached her saying Zellner had asked her 'Are you ready to die?' during the interview — Zellner felt vindicated that the question had prepared the volunteer for the real danger. She inadvertently admitted Abbie Hoffman (who was at the time 'wearing a suit and tie') and notes he is the only volunteer she knows who later complained publicly (in the Village Voice) about being mistreated as a white volunteer when SNCC moved toward all-Black organization; Susan Brownmiller (not a SNCC member) made similar published criticisms. During Mississippi Summer 1964, Zellner went to Greenwood and was assigned to one of three eight-hour shifts on the WATS line with Judy and Betty, handling press contacts, FBI work, and tracking arrests. She remained in the office throughout: 'I thought Bob was going to get killed. I thought I'm going down the road that Rita has gone.' During this period she also worked on the Harry Belafonte and Sidney Poitier benefit tour.

improvements: Note that the 'Rita' Zellner refers to is referenced without further identification in the transcript — possibly Rita Schwerner, but unconfirmed.

chapters.title: The Peg Leg Bates Meeting and Departure from SNCC
chapters.summary: After Margaret's birth in July 1965, the Zellners returned to Atlanta in fall 1965, then took a leave during 1966 when Bob managed the campaign of Robert Cook (the first anti–Vietnam War congressional candidate in the country) from New Haven. They continued to attend SNCC staff meetings during the leave. By May 1967, after substantial internal discussion that mostly excluded white staff from the substantive deliberations, the question of whether SNCC should become all-Black came to the Peg Leg Bates staff meeting. Zellner drafted a statement she still considers well-crafted: she and Bob would agree to organize in the white community but insisted on retaining staff voting status rather than being demoted to a different category. The transcript records that the meeting's minutes (which Zellner notes are in Danny Lyon's book) show the proposal was rejected by 'one vote' — meaning either it lost by one vote or they 'heard there was one vote for us' (Zellner's recollection is uncertain). They considered moving to Fairhope, Alabama (further inland than Bob's family in Daphne, Alabama, with a reputation for tolerating artists), but Bob's mother intervened, telling him directly that whatever the Zellners did, the family would 'pay the price.' Anne Braden then offered them work with the Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) in New Orleans, where they moved and worked for five years. Zellner refused to replicate the Bradens' division of labor — Anne and Carl Braden each spent six months on the road while the other minded children — viewing it as harmful to children; she worked from home while Bob traveled to Mississippi for the Mississippi Woodcutters Association and grassroots organizing. After ten years as professional civil rights workers, the Zellners 'became regular people' — Bob joined the carpenters' union and Zellner trained as a practical nurse.
related_events: Peg Leg Bates SNCC staff meeting (May 1967)

errors: The 'one vote' is ambiguous in the transcript ('we heard there was one vote for us'); preserved that ambiguity rather than disambiguating to 'lost by one vote.'
improvements: Cross-reference Robert Cook's 1966 Connecticut campaign to confirm exact district and 'first anti-war candidate' framing.

chapters.title: The 1963 Civil Liberties Workshop, White Volunteers, and SNCC's Legacy
chapters.summary: Zellner describes the spring 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop organized through the close partnership of Anne Braden and Ella Baker, both McCarthyism survivors. The workshop trained SNCC on what to do if subpoenaed by the House Un-American Activities Committee or its successors. After 'agonizing discussion' — including over Bob Moses's concern that having known communists or even white people in projects endangered local Black communities, with Herbert Lee's killing in mind — SNCC decided not to require loyalty oaths from volunteers, and that if subpoenaed, members would take the First Amendment (risking contempt of Congress and jail like Carl Braden and Frank Wilkinson) rather than the Fifth (which the media equated with guilt). Zellner believes an undercover agent must have been present and that the message reached the federal government, contributing to SNCC's never being formally subpoenaed. She tells of receiving inside information in winter 1964–65 from Barney Frank (then assistant to Allard K. Lowenstein) about meetings of higher-status civil-rights organizations including the NAACP planning to displace SNCC from leadership of a planned (and never-realized) 1965 summer follow-up to Freedom Summer because SNCC was 'too far out there.' She wrote up that information for Forman; Julian Bond later located the document. Closing the interview, Zellner reflects that SNCC achieved what in South African terms is a 'non-racial community' for those brief years, that its dissolution was a tragedy for the country, that the country remains 'profoundly racially disturbed,' and that the substrate of creativity and cross-racial solidarity SNCC demonstrated is still latent and emerges at different times. She speaks of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson at length: their initial wariness, Ruby's reputation as 'brave as a lion,' the friendship that formed when Ruby was hospitalized at Beth Israel Hospital with what was likely a virulent leukemia, and Ruby's funeral where her hair had been straightened against her lifelong practice of wearing it natural. Zellner named her own daughter Katherine Ruby Zellner after Ruby Doris Smith Robinson and after Bob's mother Katherine.
related_events: Death of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson

errors: Zellner mentions Julian Bond was uncertain about the '6,500 Black informers' figure he attributed to government sources — the summary preserves Zellner's attribution to Bond without endorsing the figure.
improvements: Add cross-reference to Cynthia Griggs Fleming's biography of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, which Zellner mentions.

chapters.title: Later Activism: Israel/Palestine and Lessons from SNCC
chapters.summary: Zellner closes by describing her current work over the past twelve to thirteen years as a Jewish activist speaking publicly about Israel and Palestine, in continuity with what she sees as SNCC's mandate to white organizers: organize in the white community on the terms of the moment's crisis. She notes that no SNCC veteran has ever told her they disagree with this work, though few have joined her. She compares this organizing to the SNCC years and finds it harder because she lacks the sustaining peer community SNCC provided — the room of brilliant, brave, and creative people where 'a person with a third-grade education might be smarter than a PhD.' She emphasizes the SNCC discipline of caring for each other and of sending organizers on rest and recuperation, an invention of James Forman's. She recounts hearing from a Black SNCC veteran (named in 'our book,' the collection she co-edited) that the abuse she suffered demonstrating with progressive Israeli Jews in Israel was worse than anything she experienced in the civil rights movement. She closes reflecting that she rarely feels she 'did great things,' but does feel that her generation 'did it' — and the lessons remain available when next needed. She also touches on the assassination of John F. Kennedy in November 1963 (she remembers picking up the phone simultaneously with Forman, who feared Lee Harvey Oswald might have been 'one of ours in the movement') and her unease at the conventional civil-rights label of 'terrorism,' which she argues should describe the lifetime of anti-Black white violence she witnessed in the South far more than its current uses.

improvements: Could disambiguate 'our book' as 'Hands on the Freedom Plow' (the book Zellner co-edited on SNCC women) which she references earlier in the transcript.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Secretary and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

evidence: Opens with full biographical context — birth date, parents' origins, family makeup (Segment 7-14, 00:00:46-00:01:43)
Chronological progression from childhood through McCarthyism (Segment 166-180, 00:14:48-00:17:03)
Builds to coherent arc through CORE workshop, SNCC entry, marriage, Peg Leg Bates departure, and present-day activism (Segment 783-2105, 00:46:46-02:43:00)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Zellner maintains a strong chronological arc from birth in 1938 through 2015 with clear thematic transitions. She occasionally backtracks (the interviewer redirects her in Segment 1442-1450 to fill in chronological gaps) but the overall structure is unusually coherent for a three-hour oral history. Some self-narrated uncertainties about specific years are clearly flagged.

most_vivid_moment.quote: I was on my hands and knees. I weighed 106 pounds. I had no purse. It was disappeared. I didn't have any shoes. I washed away. And this guy came just out of total unnecessary sadism. Hit me over the head with his stick. ... I actually saw lights going off in my brain. I'm sure that he damaged some blood vessels in my brain. And that's what I was seeing.

evidence: Rabbi Julian Feibelman (F-E-I-B-E-L-M-A-N) at Loyola/New Orleans (Segment 590, 00:43:10)
2222 Telfair Street apartment with Jane Bond (Segment 942, 01:01:17)
James Forman shaking her hand and asking 'Can you type?' (Segment 983-985, 01:03:46)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Zellner provides exceptional specificity throughout — exact street addresses, full names, dates of historical events tied to her age, technical detail about jail conditions, and reconstructed dialogue. Sensory details ('I saw lights going off in my brain,' 'gravel would be flying' describing Stokely Carmichael's arrival) reach the highest tier of vividness.

most_authentic_moment.quote: Well, I'm going to get weepy here because they're my special ones, and they're both gone now.

emotional_range: grief (for Julian Bond, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson)
indignation (at portrayals of Forman; at being thrown out of Feibelman's office)
joy (her father treating SNCC vets; meeting Paul Robeson)
self-deprecating humor (mopping incident in jail; bad driving)
reverence (for Warsaw Ghetto resistance; for SNCC peers)
vulnerability_elements: Acknowledges hoping she would be injured before going to Mississippi to avoid the trip (Segment 2196-2197)
Openly weeps over Julian Bond and James Forman's deaths
Admits she would not have gone South had she had 'family responsibilities'
Acknowledges her own role in the segregated social world she navigated ('weren't they ashamed? wasn't I ashamed?')
emotional_authenticity.justification: Zellner is emotionally accessible throughout — visibly grieving for lost SNCC comrades, willing to admit pre-Mississippi terror, and self-critical about her early racial naivete. Some emotional restraint around the deeply painful Peg Leg Bates rejection ('I was beside myself') keeps the score from reaching the 9-10 range.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Zellner is an exceptional narrator — articulate, vivid, structurally coherent across three hours, and emotionally available without performing. Her detail density (full names, addresses, dates, dialogue) is unusually high even for a Civil Rights History Project interview. The two-near-death sequences with James Forman in Greenwood and Danville are particularly cinematic. She self-monitors her uncertainty about dates, which adds credibility rather than detracting.

major_events: SNCC central office staff October 1961 onward (Segment 974-986, 01:03:17-01:04:05)
1963 civil liberties workshop with Anne Braden and Ella Baker (Segment 1966-2009, 02:19:48-02:26:25)
Mississippi Freedom Summer 1964 WATS line operations (Segment 2215-2222, 02:56:56-02:58:14)
Peg Leg Bates SNCC staff meeting May 1967 (Segment 1801-1856, 02:03:19-02:09:00)

key_figures.name: James Forman
key_figures.relationship: SNCC executive secretary and direct work supervisor 1961-1967

key_figures.name: Ruby Doris Smith Robinson
key_figures.relationship: colleague, became friends during Robinson's final hospitalization

key_figures.name: Anne Braden
key_figures.relationship: post-SNCC employer (SCEF) and prior partner in civil-liberties workshop planning

key_figures.name: Stokely Carmichael
key_figures.relationship: SNCC colleague; recruited her for The Black Panther newspaper

organizational_involvement.role: Field secretary, communications staff, Boston Northeast Regional director

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Six years on SNCC's Atlanta central staff, the period of organizational invention by James Forman; insider knowledge of the 1963 civil-liberties workshop's never-publicized decision against loyalty oaths; first-person account of the volunteer-vetting process for Mississippi Summer; drafter of the 1967 Peg Leg Bates statement; personal friendship with Forman, Bond, and Robinson.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Zellner held a central-staff position for SNCC's entire active life and contributed to several decisions that have not been adequately documented elsewhere (the civil-liberties workshop's outcome; the inside story of the volunteer-screening protocol). Her partnership with Julian Bond on SNCC press operations places her in the documentary core of the organization. Her testimony is unusually high-value institutional history.

precision_examples: February 2, 1960 — reading the Greensboro sit-in story (confirmed by interviewer, Segment 274-275, 00:26:10)
October 1961 — first SNCC office visit, one month after Forman's arrival (Segment 974-976, 01:03:17)
June 1961 — start at Southern Regional Council (Segment 972, 01:03:08)
$2,000 per car when Forman bought 20 cars for SNCC (Segment 1276, 01:24:20)
Address: 2222 Telfair Street, Atlanta — apartment with Jane Bond (Segment 942, 01:01:17)
verifiable_claims: Julius and Ethel Rosenberg execution and Supreme Court stay (June 1953)
Joseph Fichter at Loyola University New Orleans
Rabbi Julian Feibelman (F-E-I-B-E-L-M-A-N) of Touro/New Orleans congregation
James A. Moss as Queens College's only Black professor (contract not renewed)
Danny Lyon photograph of Zellner with James Forman in Danville
Constance 'Dinky' Romilly as mother of James Forman Jr. and Shaka
James Forman Jr. as Yale law professor
specificity_of_details.justification: Most verifiable claims are tied to dates and locations specific enough for archival cross-reference. Zellner acknowledges uncertainty on a handful of secondary dates (e.g., the Greenwood folk festival year), which protects the record. The detail density supports an 8 if dates were fully precise; 7 reflects the few self-flagged uncertainties.

new_information: The 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop with Anne Braden and Ella Baker is unusually under-documented in published history; Zellner says 'I'm the only one who's written about it'
Drafting of the 1967 Peg Leg Bates white-staff proposal and its rejection
Internal SNCC press-release practice of identifying Black leadership by race deliberately while leaving white as default — and the rationale
Robert F. Kennedy receiving SNCC field affidavits typed by Zellner
Barney Frank (as Lowenstein's aide) tipping Zellner off about coalition planning to displace SNCC from a planned 1965 summer follow-up
understudied_aspects: Role of Jewish women on SNCC's Atlanta central staff
Mechanics of SNCC's organizational infrastructure (the 'hub-and-wheel,' friends-of-SNCC support groups, R&R discipline) attributed to James Forman's invention
The legacy thread from SNCC organizing into late-twentieth-century Jewish activism on Israel/Palestine
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Zellner's account complicates the standard portrayal of James Forman by contesting characterizations of him as 'petulant' or 'jealous' (including in the 2014 film Selma); she emphasizes his composure, intellectual range, and protective instincts. She also softens the standard binary on white-staff departure by acknowledging she would likely have voted the same way had she been Black at Peg Leg Bates.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Highly useful for historians working on SNCC institutional history, on the Forman vs. Carmichael-era leadership transition, on internal-discipline structures, on the relationship between civil-rights organizing and broader left-wing political traditions (CP-era Smith Act prosecutions, Spanish Civil War vets, Warsaw Ghetto memory), and on women's roles in central-office work.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Zellner provides substantial new material on under-documented aspects of SNCC's internal life — the civil-liberties workshop, the volunteer-vetting questionnaire, the press-strategy discipline. Falls short of 7 only because much of her contribution is corroborative texture rather than dramatically new evidence.

historical_value.overall_assessment: This is a high-value institutional-history interview by a six-year central-staff insider who knew the key figures personally and contributed to several decisions that have not been fully documented elsewhere. Particularly valuable for any scholar working on James Forman's organizational legacy, on the post-Peg Leg Bates transition, or on the under-studied 1963 civil-liberties workshop.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Zellner moves from a 1950s 'living in books' teenager preoccupied by Holocaust memory and the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, to a frightened but determined entrant into the civil rights movement in 1960, to a central-staff SNCC organizer for six years, to a SCEF staffer in New Orleans, to a working nurse, to a contemporary Jewish activist on Israel/Palestine. She articulates explicit through-lines — particularly her Warsaw Ghetto–derived ethic that 'if you are up against the fascists... you must bring as many of them as you can,' which becomes the SNCC commitment, then the SCEF commitment, then the present-day commitment.
key_turning_points: Reading the Greensboro sit-in story on February 2, 1960 (Segment 218, 00:21:33)
Bernard Lafayette telling her 'go to SNCC' at the Miami workshop (Segment 658-661, 00:46:13)
James Forman asking 'Can you type?' on her first visit to SNCC (Segment 983-985, 01:03:46)
Peg Leg Bates rejection in May 1967 (Segment 1854-1858, 02:08:51)
Becoming a Jewish activist on Israel/Palestine roughly twelve years before the interview (Segment 2099-2100, 02:41:08)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: From a frightened, romantic teenager who fantasized about how to escape Nazi-style persecution, to a hardened organizer who has made saving the records and 'not disappearing without a trace' a core methodology. The continuity is striking — she remains the eldest-child protector throwing away the Black Book in the hallway garbage at age eight.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Yes — particularly her explicit framing that the cross-racial solidarity SNCC achieved is 'still there' and 'latent' for the next moment of crisis, and her insistence on continuing organizing into her late seventies.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is articulated explicitly and follows a coherent moral through-line from the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising to the contemporary Israel/Palestine work. The reflection is deep and self-aware throughout.

peak_moments.description: Arrest at Miami lunch counter as only white woman in the group; jail mopping incident
peak_moments.stakes: First arrest; emotional adjustment to incarceration; racial isolation in a Southern jail

peak_moments.description: Being struck on the head by police officer at Danville jail and Forman taking her affidavit
peak_moments.stakes: Physical injury; possible permanent brain damage

peak_moments.description: Danville City Hall standoff with fire hoses and 'deputized drunks'; Forman engineers retreat
peak_moments.stakes: Potential mass-casualty attack on 40 demonstrators on the steps

peak_moments.description: Peg Leg Bates vote on white-staff status
peak_moments.stakes: Six years of SNCC career; identity as Movement participant

conflict_types: moral conflict (white organizer in Black movement; Israel/Palestine work in Jewish community)
interpersonal grief (Forman's mental health decline; Robinson's death; Bond's death)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From terror (jail; Danville; pre-Mississippi anticipation) to grief (Forman, Bond, Robinson deaths) to triumph (the 1963 civil-liberties workshop holding firm; the personal continuity from Warsaw Ghetto memory to present-day activism)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Several genuinely dramatic peak moments with clear stakes; the Danville sequence in particular reaches the high end of the scale.

universal_themes: fear and the choice to act anyway
intergenerational trauma (Holocaust memory shaping civil rights commitment)
loss of comrades and the work of grief
the long arc of activism from youth into old age
the costs of work that strains family life
relatable_struggles: Navigating gendered expectations of 1950s/60s marriage and children
Balancing organizing with raising children (her refusal to replicate the Bradens' six-months-on/six-months-off model)
Finding one's people; the loss of those people
Making peace with imperfect choices (her position as a white organizer; the eventual ten-year retreat to 'regular life')
human_moments: Throwing away The Black Book to protect her younger siblings (Segment 47-54, 00:05:21)
Worrying as a 20-year-old that her time to marry would never come (Segment 286-310, 00:26:42)
Bringing her baby to work at SNCC (Segment 1729, 02:34:24)
Sitting at Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's hospital bedside laughing about gossip (Segment 2166-2170, 02:50:41)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences from any background can recognize the moral inheritance from immigrant family trauma, the fear of physical danger, the grief of losing comrades, and the question of how to keep going after a movement's high point passes.
relatable_experiences.justification: Strong universal access points via the Jewish-immigrant-family-trauma frame, the gendered expectations of 1950s adulthood, and the meditation on aging activism. Falls slightly short of 7 because some of the SNCC-internal politics (the loft vs. Raymond Street office, the central-committee vote framing) require background knowledge to fully follow.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Strong emotional engagement throughout, with multiple high-stakes peak moments and a coherent transformation arc anchored in Warsaw Ghetto memory. The grief over lost comrades (particularly Forman and Bond) is the dominant note in the second half and is genuinely affecting. Universal access points are strong via the immigrant-family-trauma and intergenerational-activism frames.

barriers: Some SNCC-internal terminology assumed without explanation (the loft, Raymond Street, the central committee, Peg Leg Bates as a place name)
Reference to Eastland hearings without context
language_clarity.justification: Zellner is a highly articulate New York-accented English speaker. Occasional SNCC insider terms (Peg Leg Bates, the loft, COFO) are used without immediate gloss. Self-contextualizes most institutions (explains what the Smith Act was, what the WATS line was, what 'friends of SNCC' meant).

high_energy_sections: Miami CORE workshop arrest and jail experience (Segment 385-455, 00:31:55-01:09:00) — vivid, fast pace
Danville sequence with two near-death stories (Segment 1162-1262, 01:17:52-01:23:24) — peak intensity
Initial February 1960 'electrified' moment reading the Greensboro story (Segment 218, 00:21:33) — strong opening hook
slow_sections: Extended chronology reconstruction with interviewer between Boston SNCC and 1967 (Segment 1700-1760, 01:55:00-02:00:25) — useful for the record but lower energy
Some recursive backtracking when interviewer prompts for missed details
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Three-hour interview sustains attention through the first two hours; modest fatigue in the final hour as material gets more retrospective. Audiences with civil-rights interest will stay engaged throughout; general audiences may benefit from chapter-level pull quotes.
narrative_flow.justification: Strong overall rhythm with multiple high-energy peak sequences. Interviewer Emilye Crosby is skilled at redirecting Zellner back to chronology without breaking flow. Some unavoidable slow sections reflect the document-the-record purpose of the project.

themes_connected: Intergenerational left tradition (CP-era McCarthyism into civil rights into Israel/Palestine activism)
Jewish-American identity in relation to anti-Black racism
The ongoing relevance of SNCC's lessons for contemporary movements (Black Lives Matter explicitly named)
Race as the central American problem ('we're going to have to have Black Lives Matter times a million')
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Zellner explicitly draws contemporary connections — Black Lives Matter, the contemporary Israel/Palestine conflict, the 2015 Charleston church shooting as terror by white Americans not labeled terrorism. Her closing reflection on the 'vacuum' of racism into which 'all kinds of people walk' translates 1960s lessons to 2015 conditions.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Strong throughout — Zellner consistently steps back to frame what specific anecdotes mean (e.g., why she still tells the Feibelman story 55 years later; what the press-release racial-identification practice taught about media literacy; what the Forman R&R discipline teaches contemporary organizers).
broader_themes_connection.justification: Zellner is highly skilled at meaning-making and connects 1960s experience to 2015 conditions explicitly and repeatedly. Falls slightly short of 6 because some of the broader-theme connections assume audiences already share her political vocabulary.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Highly accessible to civil-rights-interested audiences; somewhat demanding for general audiences due to insider SNCC terminology and assumed familiarity with mid-twentieth-century left politics. The strong meaning-making and explicit Black Lives Matter framing extend reach. Interviewer skill keeps the three-hour duration manageable.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Dorothy Zellner's three-hour oral history is among the most substantive central-staff SNCC interviews in the Civil Rights History Project corpus. As a six-year insider on SNCC's Atlanta central staff with personal relationships to James Forman, Julian Bond, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, she contributes institutional-history material that is rarely available elsewhere — particularly on the 1963 civil-liberties workshop, the Mississippi Summer volunteer-vetting protocol, and the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates decision. Her articulation is exceptional; her emotional access is genuine; her self-monitoring of factual uncertainty is admirable. The interview is high-value for institutional historians of SNCC, for biographers of Forman and Bond, and for any scholar working on the relationship between mid-twentieth-century Jewish left traditions and the civil rights movement. It will also resonate with contemporary readers concerned with the continuity between 1960s organizing and present-day activism on race and Israel/Palestine.
key_strengths: Insider perspective on SNCC central-office operations and James Forman's organizational inventions, contributed by a six-year staff veteran with personal access
Exceptional specificity in names, dates, addresses, and reconstructed dialogue — far above the corpus average for oral histories of this length
Clear and coherent transformation arc anchored in Warsaw Ghetto memory, traced from teenage McCarthyism through SNCC to contemporary Israel/Palestine activism
key_limitations: Insider terminology (Peg Leg Bates, the loft, Raymond Street, COFO) sometimes used without immediate explanation, raising the accessibility bar for general audiences
Self-flagged uncertainty on a few specific dates (Greenwood folk festival year, exact Peg Leg Bates vote count) requires reader to track which assertions are firm and which are tentative

claims.claim: Dorothy Zellner was born in New York City on January 14, 1938.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes, I was born in New York City, January 14th, 1938. The oldest of three children.

claims.claim: Her parents were Jewish immigrants — her father from Lithuania via England and Toronto, her mother from Kiev via Toronto.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father's people came from Lithuania to England. He was born in England. He emigrated at age 11 in 1910 to Toronto Canada. My mother was born in Kiev, which is now the Ukraine, but she always considered it to be Russia. A baby norm is at six months to Toronto Canada.

claims.claim: Her father became a dentist because he could not be a teacher as a Jewish person in Canada.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father was a dentist and he became a dentist because as a Jewish person in Canada he couldn't be a teacher.

claims.claim: Zellner read the Greensboro sit-in story on February 2, 1960 at a 42nd Street coffee shop counter.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went across the street. There was a coffee shop there, and I sat down at the counter. Isn't that ironic at the counter? ... I opened up the paper. ... February 2nd. Right, so the book was sitting in the first green poster in February 1st. You were about a February 2nd.
claims.rationale: Confirmed in interviewer-Zellner exchange at Segment 252-276 (00:24:54-00:26:16). The February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-in is corroborated by the ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: Zellner attended a CORE nonviolence workshop in Miami, Florida in June 1960.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we went to Miami Florida. This was core. ... And it was four months after the sit-ins. So the sit-ins were February. This is March, April, May, June. It was June, four months after the sit-ins started.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 330-354 (00:28:40-00:29:55). The chronology (sit-ins Feb 1960 + four months = June 1960) is consistent.

claims.claim: The Miami workshop included Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, Patricia and Priscilla Stevens, Angeline Butler, Jim Lowy, and Bernard Lafayette.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was, oh, just died. Rudy Lombard. There was a white guy named Hugh Murray. There was one of the twins, Pam... Patricia... Priscilla can't think of the last name. Stevens, exactly. And Angeline Butler. ... Bernard Lafayette was in this group.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 336-660 across multiple confirmations. Note: 'Pam' is mentioned but immediately corrected; the Stevens twins are Patricia and Priscilla per Segment 343-344.

claims.claim: Bernard Lafayette directed her toward SNCC during the Miami workshop.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Bernard Lafayette was in this group. And it was Bernard, who said to me, SNCC. Go to SNCC.

claims.claim: Zellner traveled with the CORE contingent to New Orleans where she helped prepare for the city's first sit-ins.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So I said, I begged them to take me with them to New Orleans. ... And I stayed at least a week, if not two weeks. And they were getting ready for the first sit-in. Which they had not had yet.

claims.claim: Father Joseph Fichter at Loyola University New Orleans agreed to help with the sit-ins.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And so I went to see this... Rather famous priest named Joseph Fichter, who was at Loyola University New Orleans. Loyola University New Orleans. ... And I met him. I told him that there were going to be sit-ins there. The first sit-in. And could they do anything? And he said, yes, we will help.

claims.claim: Rabbi Julian Feibelman threw her out of his office when she sought support for the sit-ins.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And that's where I went to see this rabbi who kicked me out of his office. ... Rabbi Julian Feibelman, F-E-I-B-E-L-M-A-N. ... And I was extremely upset when he threw me out of his office.

claims.claim: Zellner began work at the Southern Regional Council in Atlanta in June 1961, hired by James A. Moss.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So did you start working for S.R.C. like summer, 16th? It's June of 61. ... And his name was James A. Moss, M-O-S-S. He had been a professor at Queens College ... And here, James Moss had just been hired as the research director. And he saw my letter.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 821-848, 972 (00:54:47-00:56:00, 01:03:08). Note 'summer, 16th' is a transcription oddity but the June 1961 date is confirmed.

claims.claim: She first visited the SNCC office in October 1961, one month after James Forman's arrival.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went over to SNCC the first time in October of 61. Good timing. Good timing, because James Forman had been there a month.

claims.claim: James Forman asked 'Can you type?' on her first SNCC visit and put her to work typing field affidavits.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So I remember he shook my hand and he said, can you type? And I said, yes. And he said, good, we need you. ... He didn't have a small talk, but when he made a human contact with me, it was like a laser beam.

claims.claim: Forman sent SNCC field affidavits to Robert F. Kennedy.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then he sent them off to Robert F. Kennedy. And this is what's going on now, and what the idea was that there was enough on the books already in the 1957 Civil Rights Act, where all these registrars could have been sitting in jail.

claims.claim: Forman put Zellner to work with Julian Bond on SNCC communications and the Student Voice newsletter.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And maybe I said to him, I was the editor of my paper. ... and he said, good. I'm going to put you to work with Julian. ... Well I think I've been trying to remember I remember laying out dummies of the student voice.

claims.claim: Zellner married Bob Zellner in August 1963 in Atlanta.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I know that you worked in the Atlanta office through the summer of 1963. You involved Zeller and Mary in August? Yes, we were married in August. ... So I wasn't based out of absolutely yes and got married in Atlanta.

claims.claim: She was struck on the head by a police officer's club at the Danville, Virginia jail in 1963, captured in a Danny Lyon photograph.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And they came out and they turned the fire hoses on us and then later on, that's when I was hit over the head by this cop. And that's the picture that Danny Lyon took, where Forman is sitting next to me, he's taking an affidavit. I am talking about being hit over the head.

claims.claim: James Forman engineered a tactical retreat at the Danville City Hall standoff, distracting the police chief while signaling staff to leave.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He realized that we were, he realized it was going to be a disaster. And he got up and he went to the police chief and he said, you know, do you realize what's going to happen here? ... And behind his back, he sort of motioned us all to leave. ... So that's when James Forman came up and he distracted the police chief and behind his back, he went like this, this man, get up and get the hell out of there. And we did. We got up. Very dignified and we left.

claims.claim: Constance 'Dinky' Romilly was the mother of James Forman's two sons, James Forman Jr. (Yale law professor) and Shaka.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Oh yes, well, she was his Constance 'Dinky' Romilly, the mother of his two children. Both of whom are accomplished. James Forman Jr. is a professor at Yale. And the younger son, Shaka, is an actor and a teacher in California.

claims.claim: Elizabeth 'Betita' Sutherland Martínez worked with James Forman on his book during his recovery period in Puerto Rico.
claims.supporting_excerpt: She took him to Puerto Rico. And in that period, Elizabeth 'Betita' Sutherland Martínez worked with him on his book. He, he came back.

claims.claim: Zellner established the SNCC Northeast Regional office in Boston/Cambridge as a staff member (not a Friends-of-SNCC volunteer).
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was a Boston Prince of Snick and I created a Northeast regional office of Snick. And I always made it, nobody paid the slightest bit of attention but I always made a big thing about that. Because I was not, this was not a Friends of Snick group. I was a staff member and I took leadership directly from Atlanta.

claims.claim: Zellner vetted Mississippi Summer Project volunteers using a COFO-prepared questionnaire, with the volunteer pool including Abbie Hoffman.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Oh, yes. Well, we had a questionnaire which I believe was prepared by COFO. We didn't do that. ... And we interviewed every one of them personally. And usually they were two of us, a black person and a white person. ... Now, the one person I slipped up on was Abbie Hoffman. We let Abbie Hoffman through, but at that time he was wearing a suit and tie.

claims.claim: Zellner gave birth to her daughter Margaret in July 1965.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And so she was born in July. ... So she's born in July. ... I probably, it was like late May, he had already finished his semester.
claims.rationale: Birth month 'July' is stated directly (Segment 1744, 1748). The year is reconstructed from context (got pregnant 1965, returned to Atlanta fall 1965 with the baby in tow) but not stated as a single direct sentence. The daughter is also named 'Margaret' in dialogue at Segment 1512 ('introducing my daughter, Margaret, to Leslie Dunbar').

claims.claim: Bob Zellner managed Robert Cook's anti-Vietnam War congressional campaign in New Haven during 1966.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Bob was the campaign manager for the first anti-Vietnam War candidate in the country, whose name was Robert Cook. ... We were in New Haven.

claims.claim: Zellner drafted the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates statement proposing she and Bob remain on SNCC staff while organizing in the white community.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And so we talked about it, and we decided that we would agree to work in the White community. But we could not agree to being a, to surrendering our vote. In other words, we would insist on being pre-jez-regular staff members in the organization, and we would work in the white community. ... And I wrote that statement, and actually I read it now, and then I have to say it is extremely well done.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 1809-1815 (02:03:52-02:04:37). The Peg Leg Bates timing 'May 67' is stated at Segment 1801.

claims.claim: After the Peg Leg Bates rejection, the Zellners worked for Anne Braden at the Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) in New Orleans for five years.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So when it was rejected, we told Anne Braden that we were coming to work with SCEF. So we moved to New Orleans where I lived for the next 17 years. And we worked there for five years.

claims.claim: Ruby Doris Smith Robinson became friends with Zellner during her final hospitalization at Beth Israel Hospital in New York, where she died likely of leukemia.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And my parents, we stayed at my parents' house and they lived one block from Beth Israel. So, I was able to get over there a lot. ... I think it was a form of leukemia. It was very, it was virulent. I mean, I think she got sick within two years she was dead.

claims.claim: Zellner's daughter is named Katherine Ruby Zellner, after Bob's mother Katherine and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Katherine Ruby Zellner. And also, I left her father's mother, so it was after two people.
claims.rationale: Direct statement Segment 2182-2183 (02:53:21-02:53:35). Note: This appears to be a second daughter, not Margaret — Zellner had two daughters per her own statement ('two children' in Segment 1936). The transcript phrasing is somewhat compressed.

claims.claim: The 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop, organized through Anne Braden and Ella Baker's partnership, decided SNCC would not require loyalty oaths and would take the First rather than Fifth Amendment if subpoenaed.
claims.supporting_excerpt: It's the product of this very close relationship that Anne Braden and Miss Baker had. ... So we had this agonizing discussion and Bob at that time was very nervous about having white people, Bob Moses coming in because of the danger to the local people. ... But at the end people decided that they would not cooperate. This was a stunning victory.

claims.claim: Carl Braden and Frank Wilkinson went to jail for a year for taking the First Amendment before the House Un-American Activities Committee.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Or you took the First Amendment, and you had no protection whatsoever, and you could be cited for contempt of Congress. Which is what happened to Carl Brayden and Frank Wilkinson. They went to jail for a year.

claims.claim: Barney Frank, as Allard K. Lowenstein's aide, tipped Zellner off to coalition meetings planning to displace SNCC from leadership of a planned 1965 summer follow-up.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Then the winter of 64, 65, was when I inadvertently found out that Barney Frank, who was then the assistant to Allard K. Lowenstein, had been at a party. We were at a party. We heard that Allard K. Lowenstein had been at some meetings.

claims.claim: At the 1963 Greenwood folk festival, Bob Dylan performed 'With God on Our Side' and Pete Seeger was also present.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, all I remember, I remember Pete Seeger was there. Pete Seeger was in Greenwood in 1963. ... And Bob Dylan was at the festival. ... He was singing, I remember, he sang a song that I totally love and agreed everywhere. Which was With God on Our Side, With God on Our Side.
claims.rationale: Pete Seeger and Bob Dylan presence is directly attested (Segments 1400-1412). The 1963 year is stated for Pete Seeger but Zellner expresses uncertainty about the exact year for the broader festival timing in Segments 1424-1434 ('It seems to me it was after Dandel. ... It was a tree, but I'm not sure'). Demoted to partially_supported due to chronological uncertainty.

claims.claim: Zellner's father provided free dental care to Spanish Civil War veterans and later to SNCC field workers, including Bill Hansen after his jaw was broken in jail.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But about the Spanish Civil War, he always treated all of the vets that were in his practice free. For the rest of their lives, the rest of his life. And he repeated that with some of the people from SNCC. ... When Bill Hansen was in jail, don't know where I must have been Arkansas, and he got his job alvening, and his jaw was broken. ... I guess I arranged that my father would take out the wires.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 114-130 (00:10:42-00:11:52). Bill Hansen is in the ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: James Forman invented the 'friends of SNCC' support-group model and the hub-and-wheel organizational structure with Atlanta as the center.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He invented the concept of friends of. And now this is a concept that has been replicated all over the world, forget all of the United States, all over the world. ... but he also, here was Atlanta, and everything else was this wheel. Atlanta was the hub, it was the wheel. He invented the concept of support groups which did not do policy, they did not make policy. It was the center that made the policy.

claims.claim: Zellner picketed in front of the White House at age 15 the night the Supreme Court issued a stay of execution for Julius and Ethel Rosenberg.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And while we were in Washington, the Supreme Court issued a stay of execution. ... So there we were in front of the White House all night, picketing in front of the White House. And one of my memories is that along with us was picketing this young woman in high heels and it was Mary Travers, soon to be in Peter Paul and Mary, who was then a couple of years older than I was.
claims.rationale: Direct statements Segments 194-201 (00:18:39-00:19:21). Zellner is 15 at this point (born Jan 14, 1938; Rosenberg execution June 19, 1953).

claims.claim: Zellner now works as a Jewish activist speaking publicly about Israel/Palestine, in continuity with her SNCC mandate.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, since, let's see, I'm for 13 years. I've been doing this mostly. You know, that's what I do. In addition to my job is work in speaking out as a Jewish person about Israel, Palestine.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score (86) and quality score (88) fall below the 90/90 publication threshold, and the citation audit identifies three partially_supported claims that warrant human review before publication. The partial citations relate to: (1) the exact birth year of Margaret Zellner (July is direct; year reconstructed from context); (2) the exact year of the Greenwood folk festival featuring Bob Dylan and Pete Seeger (Zellner herself expresses uncertainty); (3) the naming of Katherine Ruby Zellner (the transcript phrasing is compressed and the relationship to Margaret as a separate daughter requires confirmation). None of these are hallucinations — they reflect Zellner's own narrated uncertainty or transcript compression — but the fail-closed publication gate correctly routes them to a human reviewer for clarification before the summary is published. The Smithsonian-grade standard requires that publication-grade summaries have zero unresolved citation gaps, even when those gaps reflect honest reportorial uncertainty rather than fabrication.
